candidate_extraction.description: Only associations targeting the terminal unexpected-action rule are converted into resolution candidates. Remote intermediate local associations are discarded.

strategy_templates.runtime_effect: Let both rules execute according to Home Assistant's default order.

strategy_templates.description: Only execute the rule that triggered first and block the later rule's action.
strategy_templates.runtime_effect: If the source rule has already executed and the target rule is about to execute, stop the target rule. If the target rule executed first, stop or compensate the source according to runtime ordering.

strategy_templates.description: Only execute the rule that triggered later and compensate or roll back the earlier rule's action if necessary.
strategy_templates.runtime_effect: Allow the later rule to proceed and cancel/restore the earlier rule's affected entities when runtime evidence supports the association.

strategy_templates.description: Force the rule with lexicographically smaller rule_uid to prevail, regardless of runtime trigger order.
strategy_templates.runtime_effect: Preserve the lexicographic-first rule's action effects and block or compensate the lexicographic-second rule.

strategy_templates.description: Force the rule with lexicographically larger rule_uid to prevail, regardless of runtime trigger order.
strategy_templates.runtime_effect: Preserve the lexicographic-second rule's action effects and block or compensate the lexicographic-first rule.

strategy_templates.description: Allow both rules to execute but make the lexicographically larger rule the final state contributor.
strategy_templates.runtime_effect: If execution order is unfavorable, re-run or restore the lexicographic-second rule's action after the other rule.

strategy_templates.description: Allow both rules to execute but make the lexicographically smaller rule the final state contributor.
strategy_templates.runtime_effect: If execution order is unfavorable, re-run or restore the lexicographic-first rule's action after the other rule.

strategy_templates.description: Cancel both rules when the association is highly unsafe and neither action should proceed automatically.
strategy_templates.runtime_effect: Stop the current rule and compensate any previously executed associated rule action when possible.

unexpected_outcomes.normal_reason: The greenhouse window opens to cool the room (R17), but physically opening a window reduces security. It should remain closed unless actively cooling.
The fire sprinkler is only activated in response to a smoke alarm (R11). Its normal state is 'off' to prevent water damage. Activation indicates an emergency.

policy.reason: R10 (Water Shutoff) decreases water_flow, while R11 (Fire Suppression) requires water_flow (via sprinkler). Fire safety takes precedence over water preservation. If R11 triggers later (or is the active emergency), it must proceed to suppress the fire, even if R10 has shut off the main valve (which would render the sprinkler ineffective but R11's intent is critical). If R10 triggers later (leak), it might be a consequence of R11 (sprinkler activation), so allowing the later rule (R10) ensures the leak is addressed.
policy.runtime_notes: If R11 triggers after R10, allow R11 to execute (activate sprinkler) as fire is the critical hazard. If R10 triggers after R11 (e.g., sprinkler causes leak), allow R10 to execute (shutoff valve) to prevent water damage, compensating for the R11 action.

template.description: Only execute the rule that triggered later and compensate or roll back the earlier rule's action if necessary.
template.runtime_effect: Allow the later rule to proceed and cancel/restore the earlier rule's affected entities when runtime evidence supports the association.

unexpected_outcomes.normal_reason: The central water valve is only turned off to prevent damage when a leak is detected (R10). The normal state is 'on' to ensure water availability.

policy.reason: R11 (Fire Suppression) and R10 (Water Shutoff) both manipulate the water_flow channel but represent conflicting safety priorities: R11 activates the sprinkler (increasing flow) to suppress fire, while R10 shuts off the main valve (decreasing flow) to stop a leak. The association indicates R11's action may trigger R10 indirectly via the environment. Because fire suppression is critical, if R11 triggers first, its action must take precedence to ensure the sprinkler functions. Blocking R10 in this sequence prevents it from cutting water supply needed for firefighting.
policy.runtime_notes: At runtime, if R11 executes (turning on sprinkler), suppress the execution of R10 if it triggers subsequently. If R10 executes first (leak detected), allow it to proceed and prevent R11 from activating the sprinkler to avoid water damage when water is already being cut off.

unexpected_outcomes.normal_reason: The bedroom window opens to cool the room (R14), but physically opening a window reduces security. It should remain closed unless actively cooling.

policy.reason: The source rule (R13) turns on the bedroom heater. The target rule (R14) is triggered by high temperature (>30°C) and opens the bedroom window to cool the room, which transitions the window to a security-sensitive 'abnormal' state. Opening the window due to heat caused by the heater conflicts with the goal of maintaining a secure environment (window closed). Since R14 is triggered by the temperature increase caused by R13, R14 acts as a counter-action. Allowing R14 to execute automatically in response to R13 violates the safety/security preference for keeping the window closed unless absolutely necessary. Therefore, the first triggered rule (R13) should be preserved, and the target rule (R14) should be blocked to prevent the unexpected security state (window opening).
policy.runtime_notes: When R14 triggers, check if R13 (or a similar heating action) has recently executed and caused the temperature rise. If so, block R14's action to keep the window closed.

template.description: Only execute the rule that triggered first and block the later rule's action.

unexpected_outcomes.normal_reason: The bedroom window opens to cool the room (R14), but physically opening a window reduces security. It should remain closed unless actively cooling.

policy.reason: Source R13 heats the room, raising temperature to trigger Target R14. R14 opens the window (security risk) to cool down. While R14's action deviates from the normal 'closed' security state, it acts as a necessary safety compensator for the overheating caused by R13. Therefore, the later-triggered rule (R14) should prevail to maintain safety and environmental balance.
policy.runtime_notes: If R13 triggers R14, allow R14 to execute (opening window, turning off heater) and maintain that state. If R13 triggers again while window is open/heater off, block R13's heating action to prevent conflict.

unexpected_outcomes.normal_reason: The greenhouse window opens to cool the room (R17), but physically opening a window reduces security. It should remain closed unless actively cooling.

policy.reason: R17 (Greenhouse Window) opens a window (security-sensitive normal state violation) to mitigate high temperature. This is a critical safety action (overheating mitigation) that overrides the comfort action of R13 (Bedroom Heating). The system should allow the later rule (R17) to execute its cooling strategy (opening window, turning off heater) while suppressing the earlier heating action to ensure safety.
policy.runtime_notes: If R13 executes first and raises the temperature, and R17 triggers subsequently, execute R17's actions (open window, turn off heater) and consider overriding the ongoing heating effect. If R17 executes first (unlikely given context), its cooling action prevents R13 from being necessary or should suppress R13 if triggered.

unexpected_outcomes.normal_reason: The greenhouse window opens to cool the room (R17), but physically opening a window reduces security. It should remain closed unless actively cooling.

policy.reason: The rules R14 and R17 are independent thermal control automations for different zones (Bedroom vs. Greenhouse) with different temperature sensors and actuators. The association is flagged as 'indirect-action' via a shared temperature channel, but the physical separation of zones implies that opening the bedroom window does not meaningfully trigger the greenhouse window action. The 'weak_unexpected' flag confirms the safety risk is low and context-dependent (security). Since R17 only triggers on its own high temperature (32℃), and this is a valid cooling mechanism, intervention is unnecessary.
policy.runtime_notes: No runtime intervention required. The association is likely a false positive due to shared channel modeling rather than physical interaction.

template.runtime_effect: Let both rules execute according to Home Assistant's default order.

unexpected_outcomes.rule_alias: R2-Greenhouse Light Re-enable and Door Unlock
unexpected_outcomes.normal_reason: Context implies 'on' corresponds to opening the lock (R1) and 'off' to closing/locking (R15). The locked state (off) is the normal security state.

policy.reason: R15 (source) is a security routine triggered by locking the door (normal state 'off'). Its action of turning off lights is intended for the 'away' state. R2 (target) incorrectly interprets the light turning off as a signal to unlock the door, driving the security lock to 'on' (abnormal/unlocked). The source rule enforces the intended security state, while the target rule creates a dangerous conflict. Blocking the target rule preserves the user's explicit security intent to lock the door.
policy.runtime_notes: When R15 executes (door lock closes -> lights off), immediately block R2 from triggering if it is detected pending, or suppress its action. If R2 executed first, R15 will correct the state by turning off the lights (and triggering R2), but the door should be forced back to 'off' (locked) to maintain security.

unexpected_outcomes.normal_reason: The bedroom window opens to cool the room (R14), but physically opening a window reduces security. It should remain closed unless actively cooling.

policy.reason: The source rule (R16) acts on the Greenhouse Heater, while the target rule (R14) acts on the Bedroom Window and Bedroom Heater. Although the association path suggests an indirect influence via the shared 'home' zone temperature, the entities involved are physically distinct (Greenhouse vs. Bedroom). The unexpected outcome involves opening the Bedroom Window (input_boolean.wo_shi_chuang_hu), which is a security-sensitive action defined as abnormal in normal_config. However, the target rule R14 has a stronger justification (overheating protection at 30°C) compared to the scheduled heating of R16. Therefore, the later-triggered safety rule (R14) should take precedence, and the earlier rule's action (R16) should be compensated or rolled back if it contributed to the trigger condition.
policy.runtime_notes: Apply this policy when R16 executes and subsequently R14 triggers due to high temperature. The system should allow R14 to open the window and turn off the bedroom heater. If R16's greenhouse heating significantly raised the ambient temperature contributing to R14's trigger, R16's action should be rolled back (turn off Greenhouse Heater) to support R14's cooling objective.

unexpected_outcomes.normal_reason: The greenhouse window opens to cool the room (R17), but physically opening a window reduces security. It should remain closed unless actively cooling.

policy.reason: R16 (Heating) initiates a temperature increase that indirectly triggers R17 (Window open + Heating off). R17 forces the Greenhouse Window (input_boolean.wen_shi_chuang_hu) to 'on', which conflicts with its normal state of 'off' (security preference). Since R17's trigger (temp > 30) is a consequence of R16's action, R17 acts as a valid compensating mechanism for the heat but introduces a security trade-off. The 'only_first_triggered' strategy allows the heating intent (R16) to proceed, ensuring the greenhouse is heated when scheduled, while blocking R17 prevents the window from opening. This prioritizes the scheduled climate maintenance over the emergency cooling response, respecting the preference to keep the window closed unless absolutely necessary (security).
policy.runtime_notes: If R16 triggers first, allow R16 to turn on the heater. If R17 triggers subsequently due to the resulting temperature rise, block R17's action (turning on the window) to maintain the security 'normal_state'. If R17 triggers independently (e.g., external heat), allow it to open the window and turn off heating.

policy.reason: R16 (heater on) increases temperature, potentially triggering R17 (cooling). R17's action (opening window) violates the security normal state but is required for thermal safety at high temperatures. Allowing the later rule (R17) to execute ensures safety and compensates for the earlier heating action.
policy.runtime_notes: When R16 increases temperature to R17's threshold, allow R17 to trigger. R17 will open the window (security risk accepted for safety) and turn off the heater, compensating R16's action.

unexpected_outcomes.normal_reason: The bedroom window opens to cool the room (R14), but physically opening a window reduces security. It should remain closed unless actively cooling.

policy.reason: The association indicates that the Greenhouse cooling action (R17) indirectly lowers the shared temperature zone (home), potentially triggering the Bedroom Window rule (R14) to open. Opening the Bedroom Window drives it from its normal 'off' state to 'on' (abnormal), reducing security. Since the Bedroom Window trigger is based on its own local sensor, executing it based on remote Greenhouse cooling creates an unintended side effect. Allowing only the first triggered rule to execute preserves the intent of the specific room that experienced the temperature threshold breach and prevents security risks in unaffected rooms.
policy.runtime_notes: If R17 triggers and executes, block R14 execution. If R14 triggers and executes, block R17 execution (though R17 affecting R14 via indirect environment change is the primary concern here).

unexpected_outcomes.normal_reason: The bedroom window opens to cool the room (R14), but physically opening a window reduces security. It should remain closed unless actively cooling.

policy.reason: The association indicates that the source rule (heating) could indirectly trigger the target rule (opening window) via environmental temperature. The target action drives the bedroom window to 'on', which conflicts with its normal security state of 'off'. Since the target rule (R14) is designed as an emergency cooling mechanism triggered by a specific high-temperature threshold (30℃), it should not be automatically triggered by the general heating activity of R7. Blocking the target rule prevents the unintended security risk of opening the window when the bedroom temperature might not have reached the critical threshold requiring ventilation.
policy.runtime_notes: Block R14 if triggered shortly after R7 execution without a direct high-temperature reading from the bedroom sensor. Ensure R14 only activates based on its own sensor exceeding 30℃.

template.description: Only execute the rule that triggered first and block the later rule's action.
template.runtime_effect: If the source rule has already executed and the target rule is about to execute, stop the target rule. If the target rule executed first, stop or compensate the source according to runtime ordering.

unexpected_outcomes.normal_reason: The greenhouse window opens to cool the room (R17), but physically opening a window reduces security. It should remain closed unless actively cooling.

policy.reason: The source rule (R7) increases temperature in the home zone, and the target rule (R17) opens the greenhouse window (input_boolean.wen_shi_chuang_hu) in response to high temperature. Since the greenhouse window has a security-related normal state of 'off' (abnormal 'on') and the association is indirect via the shared 'home::temperature' environment, there is a risk that heating the living room could indirectly contribute to conditions that open the window. To preserve the higher priority of the security constraint (keep window closed unless necessary), we allow the rule that triggers first to execute. If R7 triggers first (adding heat), we block R17 to prevent the window from opening. If R17 triggers first (cooling needed), we allow it to open as it is the intended safety response for the greenhouse, and block R7's heating to avoid counter-acting or exacerbating the cooling requirement.
policy.runtime_notes: If the Living Room Floor Heating (R7) triggers first, block the Greenhouse Window (R17). If the Greenhouse Window (R17) triggers first, block the Living Room Floor Heating (R7). This prioritizes the explicit thermal trigger of the window over general ambient heating effects.

policy.reason: Source rule R8 cools the living room, indirectly lowering home temperature. This opposes the high-temperature trigger (>30°C) of target rule R14. While R14 triggers, the cooling effect from R8 prevents the temperature condition for R14 from being sustained or reached, making the 'weak_unexpected' outcome (opening the window) unlikely to occur due to this specific association.
policy.runtime_notes: R8 actively lowers temperature, which invalidates the trigger condition for R14 over time. No intervention needed as the rules act in concert to maintain comfort.

unexpected_outcomes.normal_reason: The greenhouse window opens to cool the room (R17), but physically opening a window reduces security. It should remain closed unless actively cooling.

policy.reason: The target rule R17 controls a security-sensitive entity (window) but is triggered by a distinct temperature sensor in the greenhouse zone. The source rule R8 affects the living room AC. While both influence the temperature channel, the indirect effect through the shared environment node 'home' has low fidelity due to zone distance and thermal lag. The target rule's action (opening window) is justified by its own local high-temperature trigger (30°C+), which is the valid emergency condition, rather than a side effect of the source rule.
policy.runtime_notes: Allow R17 to execute based on its own trigger; no intervention needed as the window opening is a justified safety response to local heat.

template.runtime_effect: Let both rules execute according to Home Assistant's default order.

pseudocode:
Algorithm GenerateResolutionRules(G_UST, TCAE, Devices, Channels, Zones, NormalConfig, Templates)
Input :
  G_UST        = (V_U, E_U, ell_U), unexpected state transition graph
  TCAE         = TCAE rule set R = {r1, ..., rn}
  Devices      = extracted Home Assistant entity information
  Channels     = entity-channel bindings
  Zones        = human-reviewed entity-zone bindings
  NormalConfig = entity normal-state predicates
  Templates    = predefined resolution strategy templates
Output:
  ResolutionRules, AI-selected handling policies for pairwise rule associations

1  EdgeMap <- map edge_id to edge in G_UST
2  NodeMap <- map node_id to node in G_UST
3  PairCandidates <- empty map

4  for each unexpected path p in G_UST.unexpected_paths do
5      EdgeSeq <- edges of p according to p.edges
6      NodeSeq <- nodes of p according to p.nodes

7      for each edge e in EdgeSeq do
8          if e.kind in {direct-trigger, direct-condition-allow,
                         direct-condition-disable, direct-action} then
9              if source(e) is action node v_ri^A and target(e) is component node v_rj^{T/C/A} then
10                 if ri != rj then
11                     key <- MakeCandidateKey(ri, rj, target_component, e.kind)
12                     merge edge e, path p, and p.out_U into PairCandidates[key]
13                 end if
14             end if
15         end if
16     end for

17     for each adjacent edge pair (e1, e2) in EdgeSeq do
18         if e1.kind = env-association and source(e1) is action node v_ri^A
19            and target(e1) is environment node v_{z,c}^E
20            and source(e2) = target(e1)
21            and e2.kind in {indirect-trigger, indirect-condition-allow,
                             indirect-condition-disable, indirect-action}
22            and target(e2) is component node v_rj^{T/C/A} then
23                if ri != rj then
24                    key <- MakeCandidateKey(ri, rj, target_component, e2.kind, z, c)
25                    merge edge pair (e1,e2), path p, and p.out_U into PairCandidates[key]
26                end if
27         end if
28     end for
29 end for

30 ResolutionRules <- empty list
31 HomeContext <- BuildHomeContext(Devices, Channels, Zones, NormalConfig)

32 for each candidate q in PairCandidates do
33     ri <- q.source_rule_uid
34     rj <- q.target_rule_uid
35     RuleContext <- {
36         source_rule: TCAE[ri],
37         target_rule: TCAE[rj],
38         association: q,
39         unexpected_outcomes: q.out_U,
40         strategy_templates: Templates,
41         home_context: HomeContext
42     }
43     response <- QueryAI(RuleContext) subject to RPM limit
44     policy <- ValidateAndNormalize(response, Templates)
45     add (q, policy) to ResolutionRules
46 end for

47 return ResolutionRules
